2604.27872.title: Modeling Clinical Concern Trajectories in Language Model Agents
2604.27872.paper_summary: Large language model (LLM) agents deployed in clinical settings often exhibit abrupt, threshold-driven behavior, offering little visibility into accumulating risk prior to escalation. In real-world care, however, clinicians act on gradually rising concern rather than instantaneous triggers. We study whether explicit state dynamics can expose such pre-escalation signals without delegating clinical authority to the agent. We introduce a lightweight agent architecture in which a memoryless clinical risk encoder is integrated over time using first- and second-order dynamics to produce a continuous escalation pressure signal. Across synthetic ward scenarios, stateless agents exhibit sharp escalation cliffs, while second-order dynamics produce smooth, anticipatory concern trajectories despite similar escalation timing. These trajectories surface sustained unease prior to escalation, enabling human-in-the-loop monitoring and more informed intervention. Our results suggest that explicit state dynamics can make LLM agents more clinically legible by revealing how long concern has been rising, not just when thresholds are crossed.
2604.27872.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLM）代理在臨床環境中部署時，常常表現出突發的、閾值驅動的行為，對於風險累積的可見性提供有限的洞察。然後，在現實世界的護理中，臨床醫生是基於逐漸上升的擔憂行動，而不是瞬時觸發。我們研究明確的狀態動態是否可以在不將臨床權威委託給代理的情況下揭示這些升級前的信號。我們引入了一種輕量級的代理架構，其中無記憶的臨床風險編碼器隨時間整合，使用一階和二階動態生成連續的升級壓力信號。在合成病房場景中，無狀態代理表現出急劇的升級懸崖，而二階動態則產生平滑的、預期的擔憂軌跡，儘管升級時機相似。這些軌跡在升級前顯示出持續的不安，使得人類參與的監控和更明智的干預成為可能。我們的結果表明，明確的狀態動態可以使LLM代理在臨床上更具可讀性，通過揭示擔憂上升的時間長度，而不僅僅是當閾值被突破時。
2604.27872.author: Sukesh Subaharan et.al.
2604.27872.authors: Sukesh Subaharan, Venkatesan VS, Murugadasan P, Sivakumar D, Gautham N, Ganeshkumar M
